prompt: Month-end reconciliation is eating a full day of bookkeeper time, so: build `reconcile.py` exposing reconcile(ledger, statement, tolerance=3). Ledger entries are dicts {id, date 'YYYY-MM-DD', amount (signed cents, int), memo}; statement lines are {ref, date, amount, desc}. Every entry and line can be used at most once. Three passes, strictly in this order, each pass only seeing what the previous ones left unmatched: (1) EXACT — same amount and same date; (2) DATE — same amount within `tolerance` days (banks post late, default 3); (3) SPLIT — one statement line covered by 2 or 3 ledger entries that each lie within tolerance days of the line and sum exactly to its amount (rent gets paid in two transfers, that kind of thing; we cap at 3 on purpose, subset-sum madness beyond that isn't worth it). Everything must be deterministic so two runs diff clean: process statement lines within a pass by (date, ref); when several ledger entries qualify take the closest date, tie -> earlier entry date, then smaller id; for splits try pairs before triples, walking candidates sorted by (date, id) and taking the first combination that sums. Return {'matches': [{'ref','entry_ids','kind'}...], 'unmatched_ledger': [...], 'unmatched_statement': [...]} — matches listed exact-then-date-then-split (statement order within each kind), entry_ids sorted by (date, id), the unmatched lists by (date, id/ref). Duplicate ids or refs, malformed dates, non-int amounts, or a negative/non-int tolerance are ValueError; inputs must not be mutated. Acceptance = test_reconcile.py, run `python3 test_reconcile.py`; the test file is read-only as far as you're concerned.
